subtopic_detail: Epilepsy - preconception folate dose
stem: A 27-year-old woman with focal epilepsy attends her GP for preconception counselling. She has been seizure-free for 4 years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily as monotherapy, prescribed by her neurologist who has been unable to wean her without seizure recurrence. She is in a stable relationship, drinks no alcohol, BMI 24, no other medications. There is no personal or family history of neural tube defects. She wishes to conceive within the next 6 months and has not yet started any vitamins. She has read online that 'folic acid 5 mg' is recommended in epilepsy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, weight 68 kg, BMI 24 kg/m2.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable, no neurocutaneous markers, no goitre.
data_table.Medications: Lamotrigine 200 mg BD. No other medications. No allergies.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate preconception advice?

options.text: Continue valproate, start folic acid 5 mg daily and aim to conceive in 6 months
options.rationale: Valproate is highly teratogenic (NTD, cardiac, craniofacial, cognitive impairment) and is unsafe in any woman of reproductive age unless a Pregnancy Prevention Programme (PPP) is in place and no alternatives exist. Folate alone does not mitigate the teratogenic risk (RANZCOG; TGA valproate PPP; AMH).

options.text: Refer to neurology for transition to a less teratogenic antiepileptic (e.g. lamotrigine or levetiracetam) and start folic acid 5 mg daily, deferring conception until stable on the new agent
options.rationale: Valproate must be avoided in pregnancy where possible. Neurology should switch to lamotrigine or levetiracetam, conception is deferred until seizure-free on the new agent, and folic acid 5 mg daily is started preconception until 12 weeks (RANZCOG; AMH; TGA valproate PPP).

options.text: Stop valproate today without neurology input and start folic acid 400 mcg daily
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of antiepileptics risks status epilepticus, which carries higher maternal and foetal mortality than the drug itself. Transition must be supervised (RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue valproate and add lamotrigine to allow pregnancy on combination therapy
options.rationale: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk; valproate must be removed entirely if possible (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: Continue valproate and start folic acid 400 mcg daily, the standard dose for low-risk women
options.rationale: Folic acid 5 mg, not 400 mcg, is the recommended preconception dose for women on antiepileptics; even then, valproate must be replaced (RANZCOG; AMH).

explanation.summary: All women on any antiepileptic drug should receive folic acid 5 mg daily preconceptionally and through the first trimester, irrespective of the specific agent. The dose change should be made before conception because neural tube closure occurs by 28 days post-conception (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
key_points: High-dose folate (5 mg) is also recommended for diabetes, previous NTD-affected pregnancy, BMI >30 kg/m2 and some haemoglobinopathies
Lamotrigine is one of the safer antiepileptics in pregnancy but does not exempt the patient from the 5 mg recommendation
Start folate at least 1 month (ideally 3 months) preconception and continue until 12 weeks gestation
Do not stop antiepileptic therapy in a stable patient without specialist input
explanation.pearls: If a woman taking any antiepileptic asks about pregnancy, the answer to folate is almost always 5 mg, not 400 micrograms.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment
Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology - epilepsy and pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Epilepsy - valproate switch and neonatal vitamin K
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with generalised tonic clonic epilepsy is reviewed by the obstetric medicine clinic at 34 weeks. She has been on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily since adolescence and has had no seizures since her late teens. Her previous pregnancy four years ago ended in a healthy term infant who is meeting all developmental milestones, although she was not taking folate at conception that time. She booked for this pregnancy at 9 weeks and her neurologist discussed switching to a less teratogenic antiepileptic but she elected to remain on valproate given her excellent control and the late presentation; she did commence folic acid 5 mg daily from that visit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: SFH 34 cm, longitudinal cephalic, FHR 142 bpm, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 110 g/L, platelets 240 x10^9/L, INR 1.0, LFTs normal, anti-Ro negative.
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 1000 mg daily, folic acid 5 mg daily, iron, pregnancy multivitamin.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate additional measure to reduce neonatal haemorrhagic disease in this pregnancy?

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg to the neonate at birth as routine prophylaxis
options.rationale: All neonates in Australia receive IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth (or oral schedule if parents decline IM). For infants of mothers on antiepileptic drugs the IM route is specifically recommended; this is the principal evidence-based measure to prevent vitamin K deficient bleeding in this group (RANZCOG; NHMRC Joint statement on vitamin K).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment
NHMRC: Joint statement on vitamin K administration to newborns

options.text: Maternal oral vitamin K 10 mg daily from 36 weeks until delivery
options.rationale: Routine maternal antenatal vitamin K is no longer recommended for women on antiepileptic drugs in Australia; evidence does not support a clinically meaningful reduction in neonatal haemorrhagic disease beyond standard neonatal IM vitamin K (RANZCOG; RCOG epilepsy in pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment

options.text: Switch valproate to lamotrigine now at 34 weeks gestation here
options.rationale: Switching antiepileptics in the third trimester carries a high risk of breakthrough seizures and offers no benefit for the foetus given organogenesis is complete; any change must occur preconception (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology - epilepsy and pregnancy

options.text: Elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce bleeding risk
options.rationale: Mode of delivery does not alter neonatal vitamin K deficient bleeding risk; vaginal birth remains appropriate for women with controlled epilepsy on antiepileptics (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma transfusion to the neonate within 6 hours of birth
options.rationale: FFP is reserved for clinically bleeding neonates with documented coagulopathy; it is not a prophylactic measure (Royal Women's Hospital neonatal guidelines).
source_refs: Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Neonatal vitamin K

explanation.summary: Australian guidance for women on enzyme-inducing or other antiepileptic drugs is that the neonate receives standard IM vitamin K 1 mg at birth; routine maternal antenatal oral vitamin K is no longer recommended. Antiepileptic switches must occur preconceptionally, not in late pregnancy (RANZCOG; NHMRC).
key_points: Valproate has the highest teratogenic risk of common antiepileptics; counsel and switch preconception only
All Australian neonates are offered IM vitamin K 1 mg or 3-dose oral schedule
Breastfeeding is generally safe on valproate, lamotrigine and levetiracetam
Plan continuous CTG in labour for women with active epilepsy
explanation.pearls: Once she is in the third trimester, antiepileptic switching causes more harm than good. Focus on a safe birth plan and neonatal vitamin K.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Pre-pregnancy counselling and routine antenatal assessment
NHMRC: Joint statement on vitamin K administration to newborns
Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology - epilepsy and pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Neonatal vitamin K

subtopic_detail: Asthma - continuation of inhaled corticosteroid
stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 9 weeks gestation has had asthma since childhood, well-controlled on budesonide 200 mcg bd plus salbutamol PRN. Over the past month she has needed reliever 4-5 times per week with two episodes of nocturnal cough. Spirometry today: FEV1 78% predicted (baseline 95% pre-pregnancy). No infective symptoms, no exposure changes. She tells you a friend advised her to stop the budesonide because 'steroids cross the placenta'. She is up to date with influenza vaccination and folic acid.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 99% on air.
data_table.Examination: Chest clear, no wheeze, peak flow at her usual personal best.
data_table.Medications: Budesonide 200 micrograms BD inhaled, salbutamol 100 micrograms PRN.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her asthma now?

options.text: Continue current budesonide 200 mcg bd and reassess in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Her control has deteriorated (reliever >2/week, nocturnal symptoms, FEV1 drop). Continuing the same dose accepts uncontrolled asthma, which carries higher risk to mother and foetus than dose escalation (RANZCOG; TSANZ).

options.text: Step up inhaled corticosteroid (e.g. budesonide-formoterol combination) per the Australian Asthma Handbook and continue throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Uncontrolled asthma is more dangerous to the pregnancy than inhaled corticosteroids. The Australian Asthma Handbook recommends standard step-up of inhaled therapy in pregnancy, including ICS-LABA combinations. Stopping ICS is the anti-pattern to avoid (RANZCOG; TSANZ).

options.text: Stop budesonide and use salbutamol alone for the duration of pregnancy
options.rationale: Stopping ICS in pregnancy is associated with worse asthma control and adverse pregnancy outcomes including pre-eclampsia, prematurity and low birth weight (TSANZ).

options.text: Switch to oral prednisolone 10 mg daily for the duration of pregnancy
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are reserved for exacerbations, not maintenance, due to maternal side effects; inhaled escalation is preferred (TSANZ).

options.text: Switch to montelukast 10 mg daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Montelukast may be continued if it was being used pre-pregnancy but is not first-line monotherapy; ICS-based step-up is preferred (TSANZ).

explanation.summary: Inhaled corticosteroids, particularly budesonide, are the recommended preventer therapy throughout pregnancy in women with persistent asthma. Stopping preventer therapy is one of the commonest causes of exacerbations in pregnancy, which themselves drive most of the perinatal risk (National Asthma Council Australia; Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
key_points: Uncontrolled asthma is more dangerous to the foetus than asthma medication
Budesonide has the most extensive pregnancy safety data among ICS
Use a written asthma action plan and review every 4-6 weeks in pregnancy
Influenza vaccine is recommended for all pregnant women with asthma
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, continue the preventer. The foetus tolerates inhaled steroid; it does not tolerate maternal hypoxia.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - asthma in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Acute severe asthma in pregnancy - escalation
stem: A 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 28 weeks gestation presents to the obstetric assessment unit with two days of worsening cough, breathlessness and wheeze on a background of moderate persistent asthma. She had a viral upper respiratory infection last week and her partner had similar symptoms. She is on budesonide/formoterol 200/6 micrograms twice daily and reports using salbutamol every 30 minutes overnight without sustained relief. She has had two previous emergency department presentations with asthma in the last 18 months but no ICU admissions and no history of intubation. She has no fevers, no chest pain, no calf swelling, no haemoptysis and no reduced foetal movements; she received corticosteroid coverage for lung maturity two days ago at booking with respiratory. On arrival 30 minutes ago she was given continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 500 micrograms, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg and high flow oxygen titrated to SpO2 95%, with minimal subjective improvement.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 32, HR 132 bpm, BP 134/86 mmHg, T 37.4 degC, SpO2 94% on 8 L/min mask, peak flow 38% of personal best.
data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, audible wheeze, accessory muscle use, unable to complete full sentences. SFH 28 cm, CTG normal baseline 140 with variability and accelerations.
data_table.Investigations: ABG on 8 L: pH 7.36, PaCO2 36 mmHg, PaO2 78 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, lactate 3.4 mmol/L. CXR (with shielding): hyperinflated, no consolidation.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step in management?

options.text: Oral leukotriene receptor antagonist loading dose of montelukast 20 mg
options.rationale: Montelukast has no role in acute severe asthma; it is a maintenance add-on agent for chronic control (National Asthma Council Australia).

options.text: Intravenous aminophylline loading dose then maintenance infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline has a narrow therapeutic window, significant maternal and foetal toxicity and is reserved for refractory cases after magnesium has been trialled; it is not the next step (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - acute severe asthma

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 500 micrograms intramuscular every 5 minutes
options.rationale: IM adrenaline is the management of anaphylaxis, not acute asthma; uteroplacental vasoconstriction in pregnancy is a particular concern (ASCIA; National Asthma Council Australia).

options.text: Emergency caesarean delivery to improve maternal oxygenation now
options.rationale: Delivery is not the treatment for acute asthma and the foetus is currently reassured on CTG; pharmacological escalation and ICU support precede any consideration of delivery (RANZCOG; Royal Women's).

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes once now
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma not responding to back-to-back bronchodilators and IV corticosteroid warrants IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes; this is recommended in Australian asthma-in-pregnancy guidance and a normalising PaCO2 with rising lactate signals impending respiratory failure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory; National Asthma Council Australia).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - acute severe asthma

explanation.summary: Acute severe asthma in pregnancy is treated the same as in the non-pregnant adult: oxygen to keep SpO2 >=94%, back-to-back salbutamol with ipratropium, early systemic corticosteroid, then IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes if not improving. Pregnancy is a strong reason to escalate early rather than late (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory).
key_points: A normalising PaCO2 in a tachypnoeic asthmatic is a red flag for tiring, not improvement
Position left lateral from 20 weeks to relieve aortocaval compression
Continuous CTG once viable; involve obstetrics and ICU early
Document discussion about steroids for foetal lung maturation if preterm delivery considered
explanation.pearls: Do not be falsely reassured by a 'normal' PaCO2 in a severe asthmatic - she is tiring.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory - acute severe asthma

subtopic_detail: Hypothyroidism - thyroxine dose escalation on confirming pregnancy
stem: A 33-year-old woman with primary hypothyroidism after thyroidectomy for benign multinodular goitre two years ago presents at 6 weeks of a confirmed singleton pregnancy. She has been stable on levothyroxine 100 micrograms daily for 18 months with TSH consistently between 1.2 and 1.8 mIU/L. Her last TSH four months ago was 1.4 mIU/L. She conceived spontaneously after stopping the combined oral contraceptive eight weeks ago and reports nausea but no vomiting. She has no other medical problems and is on no other regular medications. She has commenced folic acid 500 micrograms daily and iodine 150 micrograms daily as advised by her GP. She has not yet had pregnancy bloods drawn and asks whether she should change her thyroxine dose now or wait for the booking blood results, which she will have done in the next few days. She has not had any change in body weight, energy, cold tolerance or constipation. There is no family history of thyroid autoimmunity.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, weight 64 kg.
data_table.Examination: Well, no goitre (thyroidectomy scar healed), no proximal weakness, reflexes normal.
data_table.Investigations: Booking bloods not yet returned. Last TSH 4 months ago 1.4 mIU/L (free T4 within range).
data_table.Medications: Levothyroxine 100 micrograms daily, folic acid 500 micrograms, iodine 150 micrograms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate adjustment to her levothyroxine?

options.text: Maintain 100 micrograms daily and recheck TSH in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Thyroid hormone requirements rise by 25-50% in the first trimester; waiting risks subclinical or overt hypothyroidism with associated neurodevelopmental impact (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society pregnancy guidelines).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Stop iodine supplementation because she is taking levothyroxine
options.rationale: Iodine 150 micrograms daily is recommended for all Australian women during pregnancy and lactation in addition to thyroxine; the two address different physiology (NHMRC).
source_refs: NHMRC: Iodine supplementation in pregnancy and lactation

options.text: Double the dose to 200 micrograms daily and recheck in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Doubling the dose risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis; the recommended increment is 20-30%, not 100% (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to liothyronine 20 micrograms three times daily here
options.rationale: Combination therapy or T3 monotherapy is not recommended in pregnancy because T3 crosses the placenta poorly and is associated with neurodevelopmental risk; levothyroxine remains first line (RANZCOG).

options.text: Increase to approximately 125 micrograms daily and recheck TSH in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance recommends a 20-30% empirical dose increase on confirmation of pregnancy in known hypothyroid women, typically achieved by taking two extra tablets per week or rounding up to the next strength, with TSH rechecked 4 weeks later (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Women with treated hypothyroidism should empirically increase their levothyroxine by about 20-30% as soon as pregnancy is confirmed, before booking bloods are back, because thyroid binding globulin rises rapidly under oestrogen stimulation. Target TSH is <2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
key_points: Practical method: take two extra tablets per week or move up to next available strength
Recheck TSH 4 weeks after dose change and every trimester
Maintain iodine 150 micrograms daily across all pregnant women
Avoid liothyronine in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Empirical dose-up first, bloods later. Waiting for the lab risks first-trimester hypothyroxinaemia.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy
NHMRC: Iodine supplementation in pregnancy and lactation

subtopic_detail: Graves disease - antithyroid choice by trimester
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman with Graves disease diagnosed 18 months ago is reviewed at her booking visit at 7 weeks gestation. Initial treatment was with propylthiouracil and propranolol, but she was switched to carbimazole 10 mg daily six months ago after biochemical control was achieved and has remained euthyroid since. Her TSH is 2.1 mIU/L and free T4 is within range on bloods drawn yesterday. She has no eye signs and no goitre on examination today. TSH receptor antibody titre four months ago was mildly elevated at 2.5 IU/L (cut-off 1.75 IU/L).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/64 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, weight 68 kg.
data_table.Examination: No goitre, no exophthalmos, no tremor, no proximal weakness.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 2.1 mIU/L, free T4 within range, TRAb 2.5 IU/L 4 months ago (mildly raised).
data_table.Medications: Carbimazole 10 mg daily, folic acid 500 micrograms, pregnancy multivitamin.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate change to her antithyroid medication for this stage of pregnancy?

options.text: Continue carbimazole 10 mg daily throughout the pregnancy and recheck thyroid function tests every trimester
options.rationale: Carbimazole/methimazole is associated with embryopathy including aplasia cutis, choanal atresia and oesophageal atresia when used in the first trimester; alternative therapy is required during organogenesis (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Refer for definitive radioactive iodine ablation this week and commence levothyroxine replacement therapy immediately afterwards
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy because of foetal thyroid ablation risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

options.text: Stop all antithyroid medication now and monitor maternal TSH and free T4 every two weeks until delivery
options.rationale: Stopping antithyroid therapy in a woman with biochemically active Graves disease risks maternal thyrotoxicosis and foetal goitre or tachycardia, particularly with raised TRAb (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil now and review at the start of the second trimester for return to carbimazole
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance is to use propylthiouracil during the first trimester to avoid carbimazole embryopathy, then switch back to carbimazole from around 16 weeks to reduce ongoing risk of PTU hepatotoxicity (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

options.text: Add propranolol 40 mg three times daily to her current carbimazole and continue both throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Beta blockade is used for symptomatic adrenergic relief in active thyrotoxicosis, not as a strategy to mitigate carbimazole teratogenicity, and the patient is asymptomatic biochemically euthyroid (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: First-trimester carbimazole exposure causes a recognisable embryopathy. Australian practice is to use propylthiouracil through organogenesis (typically up to about 16 weeks) and then switch back to carbimazole because of cumulative PTU hepatotoxicity. TRAb should be measured in the third trimester to predict neonatal thyroid disease (RANZCOG; Endocrine Society).
key_points: Carbimazole embryopathy: aplasia cutis, choanal/oesophageal atresia, omphalocele
PTU rare but serious hepatotoxicity - hence switch back at ~16 weeks
Target free T4 at the upper limit of the trimester-specific reference range
Check TRAb in the third trimester; high titres warn of foetal/neonatal Graves
explanation.pearls: First trimester = PTU. Second trimester onward = carbimazole. Always raise TRAb in the third trimester.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Thyroid disease in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: SLE - anti-Ro/La and neonatal lupus risk
stem: A 30-year-old G2P0 woman with SLE diagnosed seven years ago is reviewed in a combined obstetric medicine clinic at 11 weeks gestation. She has been in clinical remission for two years on hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily and low dose prednisolone 5 mg daily. Her one previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage at 9 weeks with no known cause identified at the time. Her most recent rheumatology bloods three months ago confirmed normal complement, negative dsDNA, and positive anti-Ro at 120 U/mL (positive >10) and positive anti-La at 70 U/mL. She has no past history of lupus nephritis, no thromboembolism and her antiphospholipid antibody screen has been repeatedly negative on three occasions over the last two years. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily and aspirin 100 mg daily for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis at 11 weeks.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, weight 62 kg.
data_table.Examination: No active joint synovitis, no rash, no oral ulcers.
data_table.Investigations: Anti-Ro 120 U/mL positive, anti-La 70 U/mL positive, dsDNA negative, C3/C4 normal, APL screen repeatedly negative, urine PCR 3 mg/mmol.
data_table.Medications: Hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily, prednisolone 5 mg daily, aspirin 100 mg daily, folic acid 5 mg daily.

lead_in: Which surveillance plan is the most appropriate addition for this pregnancy?

options.text: Serial foetal echocardiograms from 16 to 28 weeks for congenital heart block
options.rationale: Anti-Ro and anti-La cross the placenta and can cause neonatal lupus, of which congenital complete heart block is the most serious feature. Australian and EULAR guidance is to perform serial foetal echocardiography (typically weekly to fortnightly) between 16 and 26-28 weeks in anti-Ro positive women (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital Vic; EULAR SLE pregnancy guidelines).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: SLE in pregnancy
EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE

options.text: Stop hydroxychloroquine at the start of the second trimester now
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is continued throughout pregnancy in SLE because it reduces flares, congenital heart block recurrence in subsequent pregnancies and possibly the risk of pre-eclampsia (EULAR; RANZCOG).
source_refs: EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE
RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy

options.text: Empirical IVIG infusions from 16 weeks to prevent congenital heart block
options.rationale: IVIG has not been shown to reliably prevent first-occurrence congenital heart block and is not standard of care; surveillance with echo and prompt intervention if first-degree block is detected is preferred (EULAR).
source_refs: EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE

options.text: Stop aspirin because anti-Ro positive SLE increases bleeding risk
options.rationale: Aspirin is recommended for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis in SLE from before 16 weeks; anti-Ro positivity does not change this (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy

options.text: Routine empirical low molecular weight heparin from 12 weeks gestation
options.rationale: LMWH prophylaxis is indicated for antiphospholipid syndrome or significant VTE risk, neither of which apply here; SLE alone without these features does not mandate LMWH (RCOG; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies cross the placenta and may cause neonatal lupus syndromes, with congenital heart block as the most feared. Serial foetal echocardiography between 16 and 28 weeks is the surveillance standard. Hydroxychloroquine should be continued throughout pregnancy (RANZCOG; EULAR).
key_points: Risk of congenital heart block ~2% in anti-Ro positive first pregnancies, ~15-20% recurrence
Hydroxychloroquine reduces recurrence of neonatal lupus and SLE flares
Aspirin 100-150 mg daily from before 16 weeks for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis in SLE
Plan delivery and neonatology input if any conduction abnormality detected
explanation.pearls: Anti-Ro changes the foetal cardiology pathway, not the rheumatology drug list. Keep the hydroxychloroquine.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Connective tissue disease in pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: SLE in pregnancy
EULAR recommendations: management of family planning and pregnancy in SLE

subtopic_detail: Antiphospholipid syndrome - aspirin plus LMWH
stem: A 34-year-old woman is referred to the early pregnancy clinic at 6 weeks gestation. She has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages at 8, 9 and 10 weeks, all spontaneous and chromosomally untested. Six months ago she was investigated for recurrent pregnancy loss and found to have persistently positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart, with negative anticardiolipin and beta-2-glycoprotein-I antibodies. She has no personal history of thrombosis, no history of pre-eclampsia, no known autoimmune disease and no family history of VTE. Her BMI is 26 kg/m2 and she does not smoke. She is on folic acid 500 micrograms daily but is not yet on any other medication. Both she and her partner are otherwise well. The clinic discusses her diagnosis of obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome based on her clinical pregnancy loss history and persistent lupus anticoagulant, and offers a treatment plan to reduce miscarriage risk in this pregnancy. She has no contraindications to anticoagulation.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, weight 74 kg, BMI 26 kg/m2.
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable. No livedo reticularis, no joint synovitis.
data_table.Investigations: Lupus anticoagulant positive on two occasions 12 weeks apart, aCL and beta-2-GPI negative, FBC and U&E normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate treatment to start this pregnancy?

options.text: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin alone without aspirin for the whole pregnancy
options.rationale: Therapeutic dose anticoagulation is reserved for APS with previous thrombosis or current VTE; obstetric APS without thrombosis uses prophylactic dose plus aspirin (RCOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily alone with no anticoagulant in this pregnancy
options.rationale: Aspirin alone has not consistently been shown to reduce miscarriage in obstetric APS; the combination of aspirin and LMWH is the recommended approach (RCOG Green-top 17).

options.text: Warfarin 5 mg daily titrated to INR 2.0-3.0 throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Warfarin is teratogenic between approximately 6 and 12 weeks (warfarin embryopathy) and is generally avoided in pregnancy except in specific scenarios such as mechanical heart valves with careful risk discussion (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus prophylactic dose enoxaparin from a positive pregnancy test
options.rationale: Obstetric APS without prior thrombosis is treated with low-dose aspirin (commenced preconception or from positive test) plus prophylactic LMWH from confirmation of intrauterine pregnancy; this combination roughly halves the risk of recurrent miscarriage (RCOG Green-top 17; RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

options.text: Rivaroxaban 10 mg orally daily as a once daily anticoagulant option
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants cross the placenta and are contraindicated in pregnancy; safety data in obstetric APS specifically show inferiority to warfarin/LMWH outside pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome is defined by persistent antiphospholipid antibodies and a qualifying obstetric history. The recommended treatment is low-dose aspirin and prophylactic LMWH from positive pregnancy test, continued through pregnancy and the puerperium (RCOG; RANZCOG).
key_points: Diagnostic criteria require persistence of antibodies 12 weeks apart
Aspirin can begin preconception; LMWH begins at intrauterine pregnancy confirmation
Therapeutic dose LMWH reserved for APS with prior thrombosis
Avoid warfarin and DOACs in pregnancy unless specifically indicated
explanation.pearls: Aspirin + prophylactic LMWH halves miscarriage rates in obstetric APS without thrombosis.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: HIV - mode of delivery decision by viral load
stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman with HIV diagnosed three years ago attends her 36 week obstetric review. She has been on combination antiretroviral therapy (tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine and dolutegravir) since diagnosis and has been virologically suppressed throughout this pregnancy. Her viral load four weeks ago was undetectable (<20 copies/mL) and her CD4 count was 620 cells/microlitre. She has no opportunistic infections, no co-infections (HBV and HCV negative) and no other comorbidities. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated with a normally grown cephalic foetus on the 50th centile and reassuring serial umbilical artery Dopplers. She wishes to plan a vaginal birth and asks the obstetric registrar what the recommendation is regarding mode of delivery given her status and her preference. She has no obstetric contraindications to vaginal birth and there are no concerns on CTG today. She has not yet ruptured membranes and is not in labour. The infectious diseases physician has been co-managing her care.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 114/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: SFH 36 cm, cephalic, FHR 140, longitudinal lie.
data_table.Investigations: HIV viral load <20 copies/mL at 36 weeks, CD4 620 cells/microlitre, HBV/HCV negative, syphilis negative.
data_table.Medications: Tenofovir disoproxil/emtricitabine/dolutegravir once daily, pregnancy multivitamin.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding mode of delivery?

options.text: Plan an elective caesarean section at 38 weeks regardless of viral load
options.rationale: Elective caesarean is only recommended when viral load is detectable (>=50 copies/mL) or undocumented near term; routine caesarean on the basis of HIV diagnosis alone is no longer recommended (ASHM; BHIVA).

options.text: Plan a vaginal birth at term with continuation of antiretroviral therapy
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance is that elective caesarean is not required if the viral load is suppressed (<50 copies/mL) near term; vaginal birth has similar or lower morbidity with comparable vertical transmission risk on suppressive ART (ASHM; RANZCOG; BHIVA pregnancy guidelines).

options.text: Stop antiretroviral therapy two weeks before planned vaginal birth
options.rationale: ART must be continued throughout pregnancy and labour without interruption to maintain suppression; stopping risks rebound viraemia and vertical transmission (ASHM).

options.text: Plan an elective caesarean section at 39 weeks plus intravenous zidovudine
options.rationale: Intrapartum IV zidovudine is reserved for viral load >=400 copies/mL or unknown; with sustained suppression neither elective caesarean nor IV zidovudine is needed (BHIVA).

options.text: Induction of labour at 37 weeks to minimise duration of ruptured membranes
options.rationale: Routine early induction is not recommended for women with HIV who are virologically suppressed; standard obstetric indications drive timing (ASHM; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: In women with sustained HIV viral suppression (<50 copies/mL) at 36 weeks on combination ART, vaginal birth is recommended unless an obstetric reason exists for caesarean. Vertical transmission rates with suppressed viraemia are well under 1% and equivalent across delivery modes (ASHM; BHIVA).
key_points: Check viral load at 36 weeks to plan mode of delivery
Detectable VL >=50 copies/mL near term: elective caesarean at 38-39 weeks
VL >=400 copies/mL or unknown: intrapartum IV zidovudine plus elective caesarean
Continue ART through labour; avoid invasive foetal monitoring (FSE) and prolonged ROM
explanation.pearls: Suppressed viral load is the key determinant. Vaginal birth is not contraindicated by HIV status alone.

subtopic_detail: HIV - infant prophylaxis and feeding choice in Australia
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman with HIV diagnosed prior to pregnancy is reviewed postnatally on the maternity ward six hours after a normal vaginal birth at 39+2 weeks of a 3.2 kg infant. She has been on combination antiretroviral therapy throughout pregnancy and her most recent HIV viral load at 36 weeks was <20 copies/mL. The infant is well and feeding cues are emerging. She is hepatitis B surface antigen negative and hepatitis C negative. She has no other comorbidities and her partner is HIV negative and on PrEP. She lives in a major Australian city, has stable housing, a refrigerator, reliable formula supply and supportive family. She asks the midwife about safe feeding choices and what medication, if any, the baby needs. The neonatologist is also reviewing the infant and confirms the baby is well, term and not requiring resuscitation. The infectious diseases physician has been involved throughout pregnancy and is available to discuss the neonatal plan.

data_table.Vital signs (mother): BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.8 degC, well.
data_table.Vital signs (neonate): HR 138 bpm, RR 40, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 98% on air, weight 3.2 kg.
data_table.Investigations: Maternal HIV VL <20 copies/mL at 36 weeks. HBsAg negative. HCV antibody negative.

lead_in: Which combination of advice and prophylaxis is most appropriate for this infant in an Australian setting?

options.text: Formula feeding plus oral triple antiretroviral therapy to the infant for 6 weeks
options.rationale: High-risk infant prophylaxis with triple ART is reserved for infants of mothers with detectable viral load, unknown status near term or other high-risk circumstances; this infant is low risk (BHIVA).

options.text: Exclusive breastfeeding for six months plus oral zidovudine for the infant
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is not recommended in Australia because formula is safe and accessible; even on suppressive ART postnatal transmission risk is not zero (ASHM).

options.text: Exclusive formula feeding plus oral zidovudine monotherapy for 2-4 weeks
options.rationale: In high resource settings such as Australia, exclusive formula feeding is recommended because formula is safe, accessible and eliminates postnatal transmission risk; an infant born to a virologically suppressed mother receives 2-4 weeks of oral zidovudine as low-risk infant prophylaxis (ASHM; RANZCOG; BHIVA).

options.text: Mixed feeding (breast and formula) plus zidovudine to the infant for 2 weeks
options.rationale: Mixed feeding is the highest-risk feeding pattern for postnatal HIV transmission because it both exposes the infant to HIV-containing milk and disrupts gut integrity (WHO; ASHM).

options.text: Exclusive formula feeding alone with no antiretroviral prophylaxis required
options.rationale: All infants exposed to HIV in utero receive at least short-course infant prophylaxis even with suppressed maternal viral load to cover residual transmission risk (ASHM; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Australian practice for infants born to HIV-positive mothers includes exclusive formula feeding plus a short course of infant zidovudine when the mother is virologically suppressed at term. The combination drives vertical transmission rates to well under 1% (ASHM; RANZCOG).
key_points: Formula provides nutritional equivalence in a high resource setting
Low risk infant prophylaxis: zidovudine 2-4 weeks
High risk: triple ART (e.g. zidovudine + lamivudine + nevirapine) for 6 weeks plus specialist input
Test infant with HIV NAT at birth, 6 weeks and 12 weeks; HIV antibody at 18 months
explanation.pearls: Formula + a short zidovudine course is the default. Breastfeeding is not recommended in Australia even with suppression.

subtopic_detail: Hepatitis B - infant immunoprophylaxis and maternal tenofovir
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman who emigrated to Australia from a high HBV prevalence country is reviewed at 26 weeks gestation in the obstetric medicine clinic. She was diagnosed with chronic hepatitis B 8 years ago during routine antenatal screening in her first pregnancy. She is HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, anti-HBe negative, and her HBV DNA at 24 weeks is 2.3 x 10^8 IU/mL. Her ALT is 28 U/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L, INR 1.0 and her FibroScan was 5.6 kPa six months ago, consistent with minimal hepatic fibrosis. She has no co-infection with HIV, HCV or HDV on booking serology and no clinical features of chronic liver disease.

data_table.Examination: No jaundice, no stigmata of chronic liver disease, SFH 26 cm.
data_table.Investigations: HBsAg+, HBeAg+, anti-HBe-, HBV DNA 2.3 x 10^8 IU/mL, ALT 28 U/L, platelets 220, INR 1.0, FibroScan 5.6 kPa (low fibrosis).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate maternal intervention to reduce vertical transmission in this pregnancy?

options.text: Hepatitis B immunoglobulin to the mother at 28 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Maternal HBIG does not have a role in reducing vertical transmission; HBIG is administered to the infant after birth and to non-immune individuals after high-risk exposure (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Entecavir 0.5 mg daily from 28 weeks until 4-12 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Entecavir is not preferred in pregnancy because of more limited safety data; tenofovir disoproxil is the agent of choice for HBV in pregnancy (ASHM; GESA).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Chronic hepatitis B recommendations

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg daily from 28 weeks until 4-12 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance recommends maternal tenofovir disoproxil from approximately 28 weeks gestation for women with HBV DNA above 200,000 IU/mL (or HBeAg positive with high VL) because this substantially reduces the residual vertical transmission risk that remains despite infant immunoprophylaxis (ASHM; Gastroenterological Society of Australia; AASLD/EASL).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Chronic hepatitis B recommendations
EASL Clinical Practice Guideline: Hepatitis B

options.text: Routine elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce transmission risk
options.rationale: Mode of delivery does not influence HBV vertical transmission rates when standard immunoprophylaxis is provided to the infant (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Advise no breastfeeding because of high maternal HBV DNA levels here
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is safe in HBV-positive mothers provided the infant has received standard HBIG and the birth dose vaccine; high maternal DNA is not a contraindication (Australian Immunisation Handbook; ASHM).

explanation.summary: Women with HBV DNA above 200,000 IU/mL should commence tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg daily from about 28 weeks to reduce residual vertical transmission. The infant additionally receives HBIG and the hepatitis B vaccine birth dose within 12 hours of birth, with completion of the vaccine schedule (ASHM; GESA; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
key_points: Treatment threshold for antiviral in pregnancy: HBV DNA > 200,000 IU/mL or > 5.3 log10
Tenofovir disoproxil is preferred over entecavir in pregnancy
Infant receives HBIG + first vaccine dose within 12 hours of birth in Australia
Breastfeeding is safe once infant has received immunoprophylaxis
explanation.pearls: High HBV DNA in pregnancy means tenofovir for the mother and HBIG plus birth-dose vaccine for the baby.

sources.label: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Chronic hepatitis B recommendations
EASL Clinical Practice Guideline: Hepatitis B

subtopic_detail: Hepatitis C - antenatal counselling and breastfeeding
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman with chronic hepatitis C diagnosed two years ago presents at her booking visit at 10 weeks gestation. She acquired HCV through past injecting drug use and has been abstinent for three years with current support from an addiction medicine service. Her HCV RNA is 5.2 x 10^5 IU/mL with genotype 1a and ALT 32 U/L. She has no co-infection with HIV or HBV. Her liver synthetic function is preserved with INR 1.0, albumin 38 g/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L and a FibroScan of 5.1 kPa nine months ago indicating minimal fibrosis. She has not commenced direct-acting antiviral therapy. She has no other medical issues, takes folic acid 500 micrograms daily and has no allergies. She is in stable accommodation with her supportive partner who is HCV antibody negative. She asks the obstetric team about treatment in pregnancy, vertical transmission risk and whether she will be able to breastfeed her baby.

data_table.Examination: No jaundice, no chronic liver disease stigmata.
data_table.Investigations: HCV RNA 5.2 x 10^5 IU/mL, genotype 1a, ALT 32 U/L, albumin 38 g/L, INR 1.0, platelets 220, FibroScan 5.1 kPa.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice to give now?

options.text: Defer direct-acting antiviral therapy and plan breastfeeding once nipples intact
options.rationale: Direct-acting antivirals are not yet routinely recommended in pregnancy in Australia outside trial settings; treatment is deferred to postpartum. Breastfeeding is encouraged in HCV-positive women unless nipples are cracked or bleeding (ASHM; GESA; Australian Breastfeeding Association).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Hepatitis C consensus statement

options.text: Start sofosbuvir/velpatasvir at 12 weeks and continue for 12 weeks total
options.rationale: DAA therapy is not routinely recommended in pregnancy in Australia at present; pharmacokinetic and safety data are limited and treatment is deferred unless within a research protocol (ASHM; GESA).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Hepatitis C consensus statement

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce vertical transmission
options.rationale: Mode of delivery does not reduce HCV vertical transmission rates and elective caesarean is not indicated for HCV alone (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Advise no breastfeeding because of detectable HCV RNA in her serum
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is safe in HCV-positive mothers because HCV is not transmitted through intact, healthy lactation; the standard advice is to pause feeding from a side with cracked or bleeding nipples (ASHM).

options.text: Test the newborn with HCV antibody within 48 hours of delivery here
options.rationale: Passively acquired maternal anti-HCV persists for up to 18 months in infants and renders early antibody testing uninformative; HCV PCR at 2 and 6 months or antibody after 18 months is the recommended schedule (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C are deferred until after pregnancy in current Australian practice. Vertical transmission is approximately 5-6% and is not reduced by elective caesarean; breastfeeding is safe except when nipples are cracked or bleeding (ASHM; GESA).
key_points: Treat HCV postpartum, ideally before any subsequent pregnancy
No role for routine elective caesarean section for HCV alone
Infant follow-up: HCV PCR at 2 and 6 months or antibody from 18 months
Higher transmission with HIV co-infection - test for both at booking
explanation.pearls: HCV currently waits. The biggest risk factor for vertical transmission you can act on antenatally is HIV co-infection.

sources.label: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): Hepatitis C consensus statement

subtopic_detail: ITP - platelet threshold for neuraxial anaesthesia
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman with ITP diagnosed three years ago presents to obstetric day assessment at 36 weeks gestation. Her ITP has previously responded to oral prednisolone but she has been off all therapy for the past 12 months with platelet counts between 90 and 110 x10^9/L. Today her routine bloods show a platelet count of 72 x10^9/L, down from 95 x10^9/L two weeks ago. She has no bleeding, no petechiae and no bruising, and the rest of her FBC and a peripheral blood film show isolated thrombocytopenia consistent with her known ITP. She has no signs of pre-eclampsia, no other medical problems and no allergies. She is on iron supplementation only and her booking HIV, HBV and HCV serology was negative. She is keen to have an epidural in labour. The obstetric anaesthetist and obstetric haematologist are reviewing her at 36 weeks and discussing a plan to optimise her platelets prior to expected delivery.

data_table.Examination: No petechiae or bruising. SFH 36 cm, cephalic.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 72 x10^9/L (95 two weeks ago), Hb 116 g/L, WCC 7.0, INR 1.0, blood film: isolated thrombocytopenia consistent with ITP.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan with respect to neuraxial anaesthesia and delivery?

options.text: No treatment required because platelets above 50 x10^9/L allow epidural use
options.rationale: A platelet threshold of >50 x10^9/L is acceptable for vaginal birth without instrumentation but not for neuraxial anaesthesia, which generally requires >70-80 x10^9/L (ASH 2019; KEMH).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone aiming for platelets above 80 x10^9/L by labour
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance aims for platelets above 80 x10^9/L for neuraxial anaesthesia and above 50 x10^9/L for vaginal delivery in ITP; first-line therapy is oral prednisolone, escalating to IVIG if response is inadequate or delivery is imminent (RANZCOG; ASH ITP guideline; KEMH).

options.text: Plan immediate platelet transfusion to bring the count above 100 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Platelet transfusions are short-lived in ITP because of ongoing antibody-mediated destruction and are reserved for active bleeding or imminent surgery, not antenatal optimisation (ASH 2019).

options.text: Start eltrombopag 50 mg orally daily and continue throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Thrombopoietin receptor agonists are reserved for refractory ITP unresponsive to corticosteroids and IVIG; safety data in pregnancy are limited (Therapeutic Guidelines; ASH 2019).

options.text: Splenectomy after delivery is no longer indicated for chronic ITP
options.rationale: While the option contains a partially correct statement, it does not address the immediate question of optimising platelets for delivery; splenectomy is still considered for selected refractory chronic ITP (ASH 2019).

explanation.summary: In ITP in late pregnancy the team should target platelets above 50 x10^9/L for vaginal birth, above 70-80 x10^9/L for epidural and above 80 x10^9/L for caesarean section. Oral prednisolone is first line with IVIG used for steroid failure or rapid response need (RANZCOG; ASH 2019; KEMH).
key_points: First line: oral prednisolone 20-30 mg daily aiming for response in 1-2 weeks
Second line: IVIG 1 g/kg for faster onset close to delivery
Avoid scalp electrodes, foetal blood sampling, vacuum and difficult forceps in neonates at risk of thrombocytopenia
Check neonatal platelet count - mother's count does not predict baby's
explanation.pearls: Memorise 50/80: 50 for vaginal birth, 80 for epidural/caesarean. Prednisolone first, IVIG if you are running out of time.

subtopic_detail: IBD - medication safety in pregnancy
stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 woman with Crohn disease presents for preconception counselling. She has been in clinical and biochemical remission for 18 months on azathioprine 150 mg daily and adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously every two weeks. Three years ago, before stable remission, she had a flare during which she received a 6-month course of methotrexate that was subsequently stopped. She is now planning pregnancy in the next three months. She has no other medical problems and no allergies. Her gastroenterologist is part of the discussion and her preconception bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP and faecal calprotectin <50 micrograms/g) are reassuring. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily. She asks specifically which of her current and past medications might need to change before conception. Her vaccinations including MMR, varicella and pertussis are up to date and her partner is well with no relevant medical history.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, weight 60 kg.
data_table.Examination: No active perianal disease. Soft abdomen. No mucocutaneous EIM.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U&E, LFTs normal, CRP 2 mg/L, faecal calprotectin <50 micrograms/g.
data_table.Medications: Azathioprine 150 mg daily, adalimumab 40 mg SC fortnightly, folic acid 5 mg daily.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication advice for preconception?

options.text: Stop azathioprine preconception and switch to oral budesonide as her sole maintenance therapy for the pregnancy
options.rationale: Azathioprine is recommended to continue throughout pregnancy to maintain remission; switching to budesonide is not equivalent maintenance therapy (GESA; ECCO).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): IBD in pregnancy

options.text: Continue azathioprine and adalimumab; ensure methotrexate has been stopped for at least 3-6 months
options.rationale: Thiopurines and most anti-TNF agents are continued through pregnancy because flare risk outweighs medication risk and both are considered low risk; methotrexate is teratogenic and must be stopped for 3-6 months preconception in both partners (GESA; ECCO IBD pregnancy consensus; Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): IBD in pregnancy

options.text: Stop adalimumab at conception and avoid all biologic therapy for the duration of the pregnancy
options.rationale: Most anti-TNF biologics, including adalimumab, are continued at least through to 24-30 weeks (some continue to delivery) because flare during pregnancy worsens outcomes more than the drug (GESA; ECCO).
source_refs: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): IBD in pregnancy

options.text: Restart methotrexate 15 mg weekly if any biochemical evidence of flare during this pregnancy emerges
options.rationale: Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy; both partners should stop methotrexate 3-6 months before conception (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

options.text: Switch azathioprine to mycophenolate mofetil preconception for stronger maintenance immunosuppression during pregnancy
options.rationale: Mycophenolate is teratogenic (cleft palate, ear malformations) and contraindicated in pregnancy; azathioprine remains the thiopurine of choice (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

explanation.summary: Most IBD medications including 5-ASAs, thiopurines and anti-TNF biologics are continued in pregnancy because relapse worsens maternal and foetal outcomes more than the drug. Methotrexate and mycophenolate are absolutely contraindicated and must be stopped well in advance of conception (GESA; ECCO).
key_points: Azathioprine: continue; check TPMT once; routine monitoring of bloods
Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab): continue; consider holding final dose if active disease is well controlled to limit transplacental transfer late in pregnancy
Avoid live vaccines in the infant for 6 months after intrauterine biologic exposure (e.g. rotavirus, BCG)
Methotrexate teratogenicity: aim 3-6 months washout in both partners
explanation.pearls: The general rule: most IBD drugs continue. The exceptions are methotrexate and mycophenolate, both teratogenic.

sources.label: Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA): IBD in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Type 1 diabetes - preconception HbA1c target
stem: A 26-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes diagnosed at age 11 attends preconception clinic with her partner. She uses multiple daily injections (insulin glargine 18 units nocte and insulin aspart with meals) with continuous glucose monitoring. Her most recent HbA1c is 8.4% (68 mmol/mol), down from 9.2% (77 mmol/mol) six months ago. She has background diabetic retinopathy on screening last year but no proliferative changes, urine ACR 1.8 mg/mmol, normal renal function and no neuropathy. She has no other medical problems, no allergies and is not on any other regular medication. She is taking a standard prenatal multivitamin but no separate folic acid supplement. She has been smoke free for two years and drinks no alcohol. She tells you she would like to start trying to conceive in the next six months and asks whether her current glucose control and supplementation are adequate. Her partner is well.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, BMI 26 kg/m2.
data_table.Examination: No peripheral neuropathy, monofilament intact, fundoscopy: background retinopathy.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.4% (68 mmol/mol), urine ACR 1.8 mg/mmol, eGFR >90, TSH normal.
data_table.Medications: Insulin glargine 18 units nocte, insulin aspart with meals, standard prenatal multivitamin.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her preconception care?

options.text: Switch from insulin to metformin 1 g twice daily before conception and continue throughout the pregnancy
options.rationale: Type 1 diabetes is not treated with metformin substitution; intensive insulin therapy with adjustments is the mainstay during pregnancy (ADIPS).

options.text: Reassure that the current HbA1c of 8.4% is acceptable for proceeding immediately to conception attempts
options.rationale: HbA1c 8.4% is associated with markedly elevated rates of major congenital anomalies, miscarriage and stillbirth; conception is recommended to be deferred until <6.5% or below 7.0% when safely achievable (ADIPS).

options.text: Intensify glucose management aiming for HbA1c below 6.5% and start folic acid 5 mg daily
options.rationale: ADIPS, RANZCOG and the Australian Diabetes Society recommend HbA1c below 6.5% (48 mmol/mol) preconception when achievable without significant hypoglycaemia, plus folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 1-3 months before conception in pre-existing diabetes (ADIPS; RANZCOG; Australian Diabetes Society pre-existing diabetes guideline).
source_refs: Australian Diabetes Society: Diabetes management in pregnancy

options.text: Advise indefinitely against pregnancy because of background diabetic retinopathy and the current HbA1c level
options.rationale: Background retinopathy is not a contraindication to pregnancy; preconception optimisation and ophthalmology surveillance are appropriate (ADIPS; RANZCOG).

options.text: Start a SGLT2 inhibitor such as empagliflozin preconception to reduce HbA1c rapidly before conception attempts
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors carry diabetic ketoacidosis risk and are contraindicated in pregnancy and in type 1 diabetes generally without specialist input (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Preconception care in pre-existing type 1 diabetes targets HbA1c below 6.5% where safely achievable, folic acid 5 mg daily, retinal and renal review, and review of all non-insulin medications. Achieving these targets substantially reduces congenital anomaly and miscarriage rates (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
key_points: Target HbA1c <6.5% (or <7.0% if hypoglycaemia limits)
Folic acid 5 mg daily for diabetes is one of the standard 5 mg indications
Stop teratogenic agents preconception: ACE-i/ARB, statin, SGLT2i
Retinal screening before pregnancy and at least once per trimester
explanation.pearls: T1DM preconception target HbA1c is <6.5% and folate is 5 mg, not 400 micrograms.

sources.label: Australian Diabetes Society: Diabetes management in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum glucose targets in pre-existing diabetes
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with type 1 diabetes for 15 years is admitted in spontaneous labour at 38+3 weeks. She has been on insulin pump therapy throughout pregnancy with an HbA1c at 36 weeks of 5.9% (41 mmol/mol). Growth has tracked along the 80th centile with an estimated foetal weight 3.7 kg one week ago. There is no polyhydramnios. She has no diabetic complications other than background retinopathy and no pre-eclampsia. She is currently 4 cm dilated, contracting regularly and requesting an epidural. On admission her capillary blood glucose is 8.2 mmol/L and she has trace ketones on urine dip. The midwifery team is asking the obstetric registrar about the appropriate glucose target during labour and the optimal way to manage her insulin from now on. She has no allergies and no other regular medications. The neonatal team has been notified to attend delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm/fully effaced, station -2, contractions 3:10, CTG normotypical baseline 140.
data_table.Investigations: CBG 8.2 mmol/L, urine ketones trace, U&E normal, recent HbA1c 5.9%.
data_table.Medications: Insulin pump (basal aspart). Folic acid 5 mg until 12 weeks (stopped), iodine.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum glucose management?

options.text: Continue subcutaneous insulin pump unchanged with 4-hourly capillary glucose monitoring
options.rationale: Pump therapy may continue intrapartum only with strict hourly monitoring and clear failure protocols; 4-hourly monitoring is too infrequent for the labouring T1DM patient (ADIPS; RANZCOG).

options.text: Switch to variable-rate IV insulin infusion targeting CBG 4-7 mmol/L
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance is to target intrapartum capillary glucose 4-7 mmol/L, monitored hourly, using a variable rate intravenous insulin infusion with dextrose where needed; this reduces neonatal hypoglycaemia (ADIPS; RANZCOG; NICE Diabetes in Pregnancy).

options.text: Stop all insulin in labour and recommence after delivery of the placenta
options.rationale: Type 1 diabetes patients must not have insulin withheld - stopping basal insulin precipitates DKA (Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology).

options.text: Aim for capillary glucose 8-10 mmol/L to reduce maternal hypoglycaemia risk
options.rationale: Higher intrapartum targets are associated with increased neonatal hypoglycaemia from foetal hyperinsulinism; 4-7 mmol/L is the recommended range (ADIPS).

options.text: Give a single dose of long-acting insulin glargine 20 units now
options.rationale: Long-acting insulin is poorly titratable in labour; variable rate IV insulin allows minute-to-minute adjustment to maintain the narrow target (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: Intrapartum care of T1DM uses a variable rate IV insulin infusion with hourly capillary glucose monitoring, targeting 4-7 mmol/L to reduce neonatal hypoglycaemia. After delivery of the placenta insulin requirements fall sharply and infusion rates must be halved or stopped to avoid maternal hypoglycaemia (ADIPS; RANZCOG).
key_points: Target intrapartum CBG 4-7 mmol/L
Hourly CBG, ketone check if CBG >12 or symptoms
Halve infusion or revert to subcut basal/bolus after placenta delivered
Neonatal CBG checks per local pathway (e.g. before second feed, then 3-4 hourly)
explanation.pearls: 4 to 7 in labour, halve the insulin after the placenta.

subtopic_detail: Mechanical mitral valve - anticoagulation by trimester
stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 woman with a bileaflet mechanical mitral valve replacement for rheumatic heart disease attends preconception clinic with her cardiologist and an obstetric physician. Her rheumatic disease was acquired in childhood before she immigrated to Australia at age 12; surveillance now demonstrates only the prosthetic valve as a residual lesion. She has been on warfarin 4 mg daily with stable INR 2.5-3.5 since her surgery five years ago, monitored at a community anticoagulation clinic, with no thrombotic or bleeding events. She is currently in sinus rhythm, NYHA functional class I with no exertional symptoms during stair climbing, and her most recent transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal mechanical valve function with no regurgitation, no left atrial enlargement, no pulmonary hypertension and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%.

data_table.Examination: Mechanical S2, no murmurs, no signs of failure, NYHA I.
data_table.Investigations: INR 2.8, LVEF 60%, normal valve function on TTE.
data_table.Medications: Warfarin 4 mg daily, folic acid 5 mg daily.

lead_in: Which anticoagulation strategy is most appropriate to plan from conception through to delivery?

options.text: Use therapeutic-dose enoxaparin with anti-Xa monitoring for the entire pregnancy from positive test until 36 weeks then switch to unfractionated heparin
options.rationale: LMWH throughout is associated with a higher rate of valve thrombosis than warfarin-containing strategies, particularly in mechanical mitral valves; ESC reserves this strategy for women who decline warfarin after counselling (ESC 2018).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

options.text: Continue warfarin at the same dose from conception through to elective induction at term to minimise mechanical valve thrombosis risk throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Warfarin throughout pregnancy minimises maternal thromboembolism but at the cost of warfarin embryopathy (6-12 weeks) and foetal bleeding at delivery; pure warfarin throughout is rarely chosen without a structured discussion of trade-offs (ESC 2018).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to therapeutic-dose enoxaparin from positive pregnancy test, return to warfarin from 13-36 weeks, switch back to LMWH from 36 weeks to delivery
options.rationale: ESC and RANZCOG guidance recommend dose-adjusted LMWH (with anti-Xa monitoring) in the first trimester to avoid warfarin embryopathy when the daily warfarin dose is moderate to high, warfarin in the second and early third trimester for stable INR and lowest thrombotic risk on the valve, then LMWH again from 36 weeks to allow safe neuraxial anaesthesia and delivery (ESC 2018 Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy; RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy
Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

options.text: Switch to rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily preconception and continue this direct oral anticoagulant throughout the entire pregnancy and puerperium
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants are contraindicated in pregnancy and ineffective in mechanical valves; they have no role here (ESC 2018; AMH).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

options.text: Stop all anticoagulation in the first trimester to avoid warfarin embryopathy and reintroduce therapeutic LMWH at 14 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Mechanical mitral valves are at very high thrombotic risk and never go unanticoagulated; stopping in any trimester is contraindicated (ESC 2018).
source_refs: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Anticoagulation in mechanical heart valves in pregnancy balances maternal valve thrombosis (best minimised with warfarin) and foetal embryopathy/bleeding (worse with warfarin). A common sequenced strategy is LMWH in the first trimester (especially if warfarin dose >5 mg), warfarin in mid-pregnancy, and LMWH again from 36 weeks for safe peridelivery management (ESC 2018; RANZCOG).
key_points: Daily warfarin dose <5 mg: lower embryopathy risk so warfarin throughout is more acceptable
Therapeutic LMWH requires anti-Xa monitoring (peak 4 hours post dose)
Plan delivery on LMWH; convert to IV unfractionated heparin 36 hours before and stop 4-6 hours before neuraxial
Restart anticoagulation 6-12 hours after vaginal birth or 12-24 hours after caesarean if no bleeding
explanation.pearls: Mechanical valves are anticoagulation jigsaws: never stop entirely, plan the trimester swaps in advance.

sources.label: ESC Guideline 2018: Cardiovascular disease in pregnancy
Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - anticoagulation in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Postnatal VTE prophylaxis - risk-based dosing
stem: A 36-year-old G3P2 woman delivers a healthy 3.6 kg infant by emergency caesarean section at 39+1 weeks for failure to progress in the first stage of labour. Her BMI on booking was 33 kg/m2 and her current weight is 92 kg with proportionate gestational weight gain. She is otherwise well with no personal or family history of thrombosis, no known thrombophilia and no immobilising disease. The caesarean was technically straightforward with estimated blood loss 600 mL, no transfusion required and no postpartum haemorrhage. She is now on the postnatal ward six hours after delivery, observations stable, mobilising with assistance, no signs of bleeding, lochia normal and the wound dressing dry.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, dressing dry, lochia normal, calves soft and non-tender, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L (booking 122), platelets 240 x10^9/L, U&E normal, INR 1.0.
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol PRN, ibuprofen PRN. No anticoagulant yet.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate VTE prophylaxis plan for this patient?

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily orally for 7 days post-discharge
options.rationale: DOACs are not recommended for postpartum thromboprophylaxis in Australia, particularly in women who are breastfeeding; LMWH is preferred (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Mechanical thromboprophylaxis with TED stockings alone for duration of admission
options.rationale: Mechanical prophylaxis alone is insufficient for a patient with multiple risk factors plus emergency caesarean; pharmacological prophylaxis is required (RCOG Green-top 37a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 90 mg twice daily for 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Therapeutic dose anticoagulation is reserved for treatment of confirmed VTE or for very high-risk prophylaxis (e.g. recent VTE, antiphospholipid syndrome with prior thrombosis); this patient meets prophylactic, not therapeutic, criteria (RCOG Green-top 37a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for at least 10 days postoperatively
options.rationale: Emergency caesarean plus age >=35 plus BMI >=30 plus parity >=3 each adds points on Australian and RCOG postnatal VTE risk scoring; this combination indicates pharmacological prophylaxis for at least 10 days postpartum. Enoxaparin 40 mg daily is appropriate at her weight (RCOG Green-top 37a; RANZCOG; KEMH).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium
RANZCOG: Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and the puerperium
KEMH: VTE prophylaxis in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg orally daily for 6 weeks postpartum as VTE prophylaxis
options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended as routine postnatal VTE prophylaxis in this risk profile; LMWH provides the most evidence-based reduction in VTE postpartum (RCOG Green-top 37a).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium

explanation.summary: Postpartum VTE risk assessment uses cumulative risk factors. Emergency caesarean alone earns prophylaxis for at least 10 days; combined with BMI >=30, age >=35 and parity >=3 the indication is robust. Standard agent is prophylactic-dose LMWH starting 6-12 hours after delivery if no bleeding (RCOG; RANZCOG; KEMH).
key_points: Score in late pregnancy and again postnatally; rescore if status changes
Standard prophylactic enoxaparin dose 40 mg daily for weight 50-90 kg; weight-band adjust outside this range
Continue 10 days postpartum at minimum; 6 weeks if previous VTE, persistent risk factors or thrombophilia
LMWH compatible with breastfeeding
explanation.pearls: Emergency caesarean plus any second risk factor almost always equals 10 days of enoxaparin.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top 37a: Thrombosis and embolism during pregnancy and the puerperium
RANZCOG: Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy and the puerperium
KEMH: VTE prophylaxis in pregnancy and the puerperium

subtopic_detail: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy - UDCA and delivery timing
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman at 35+2 weeks presents with two weeks of progressive generalised itching that is worst on the palms and soles and worse at night. She has no rash, no jaundice and no right upper quadrant pain. She has no significant past medical history and her booking bloods including viral screen were normal. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated. Her LFTs today show ALT 110 U/L (ULN 35), AST 88 U/L (ULN 35), bilirubin 12 micromol/L, GGT 60 U/L, ALP 280 U/L, and total fasting bile acids 56 micromol/L. Her INR is 1.0 and platelets are 220 x10^9/L. She has no signs of pre-eclampsia, urine PCR is 4 mg/mmol and her CTG is reassuring. Liver ultrasound shows no gallstones, normal hepatic parenchyma and patent portal vessels. She has had no other liver insults and is on no regular medications other than a pregnancy multivitamin.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Excoriations on palms, soles, abdomen. No jaundice. SFH 35 cm. CTG normotypical.
data_table.Investigations: ALT 110 U/L, AST 88 U/L, GGT 60 U/L, ALP 280 U/L, bilirubin 12 micromol/L, fasting bile acids 56 micromol/L, INR 1.0, platelets 220, urine PCR 4 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combined plan for medication and timing of delivery?

options.text: Ursodeoxycholic acid 500 mg twice daily and plan delivery at 37-38 weeks
options.rationale: Ursodeoxycholic acid relieves maternal symptoms and is the recommended pharmacological treatment for intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy. For moderate disease (bile acids 40-99 micromol/L) Australian and RCOG guidance recommends planning delivery at 37-38+6 weeks to reduce stillbirth risk (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 43 ICP 2022; Royal Women's Hospital Vic).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy 2022
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Obstetric cholestasis

options.text: Topical emollients only and plan delivery at term 39-40 weeks
options.rationale: Emollients can adjunctively soothe itch but do not address the disease; with bile acids 56 micromol/L delivery should not be deferred to 39-40 weeks because stillbirth risk rises (RCOG ICP 2022).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy 2022

options.text: Cholestyramine 4 g three times daily and plan immediate delivery now
options.rationale: Cholestyramine is no longer first line in Australia and risks vitamin K malabsorption; immediate delivery at 35+2 weeks is reserved for severe disease (bile acids >=100 micromol/L) or other indications (RCOG ICP 2022; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy 2022

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 12 mg daily for 7 days as definitive symptomatic therapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not first-line treatment for ICP; UDCA is the principal pharmacological agent (RCOG ICP 2022).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy 2022

options.text: Antihistamine therapy with chlorphenamine and expectant management to 40 weeks
options.rationale: Antihistamines are only modestly effective for ICP-related pruritus and do not address stillbirth risk; expectant management to 40 weeks is not appropriate at this bile acid level (RCOG ICP 2022).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy 2022

explanation.summary: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is suggested by pruritus without rash, deranged transaminases and a raised total bile acid; bile acid level guides delivery timing. UDCA improves maternal pruritus and is standard of care. Delivery at 37-38+6 weeks is appropriate for bile acids 40-99 micromol/L; consider earlier delivery for >=100 micromol/L (RANZCOG; RCOG ICP 2022).
key_points: Stillbirth risk relates predominantly to peak bile acid concentration
UDCA 10-15 mg/kg/day in divided doses, typically 500 mg BD
Bile acids 40-99 micromol/L: deliver 37-38+6 weeks; >=100 micromol/L: consider 35-36 weeks
ICP usually resolves within days of delivery; consider recurrence in future pregnancies
explanation.pearls: UDCA helps the itch and bile acid level drives the calendar.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy 2022
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Obstetric cholestasis

subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy versus HELLP
stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 36+4 weeks presents to the maternity assessment unit with 36 hours of nausea, vomiting and right upper quadrant pain. She is increasingly fatigued and her partner reports she has been progressively confused for the last few hours, with intermittent disorientation to time and place. There is no headache, no visual disturbance, no vaginal bleeding and she reports no reduced foetal movements but feels generally unwell. On arrival her BP is 138/88 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 98% on air and she appears jaundiced with mild asterixis. Her abdomen is tender in the right upper quadrant without rebound; CTG shows a normotypical baseline of 145 bpm with reduced variability. Her bedside capillary glucose is 2.6 mmol/L. Urgent bloods reveal an ALT of 540 U/L, AST 520 U/L, bilirubin 78 micromol/L, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 95 x10^9/L, ammonia 92 micromol/L, urate 0.46 mmol/L and creatinine 138 micromol/L. Lactate is 4.2 mmol/L and the blood film shows no schistocytes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/88 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 98%, CBG 2.6 mmol/L.
data_table.Examination: Jaundice, mild asterixis, RUQ tenderness, GCS 14, no peripheral oedema, CTG reduced variability.
data_table.Investigations: ALT 540 U/L, AST 520 U/L, bilirubin 78 micromol/L, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 95 x10^9/L, ammonia 92 micromol/L, urate 0.46 mmol/L, creatinine 138 micromol/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, urine PCR 28 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours acute fatty liver of pregnancy over HELLP syndrome?

options.text: Mild hypertension with a urine PCR above 30 mg/mmol
options.rationale: Hypertension and proteinuria are core features of pre-eclampsia/HELLP and are less characteristic of AFLP, where they may be absent or mild (RANZCOG).

options.text: Right upper quadrant pain with raised transaminases above 500 U/L
options.rationale: RUQ pain and transaminitis are shared between HELLP and AFLP; this feature alone does not discriminate (Swansea criteria).
source_refs: Swansea criteria (Knight et al.) for acute fatty liver of pregnancy

options.text: Platelet count below 100 x10^9/L on this presentation
options.rationale: Thrombocytopenia <100 x10^9/L is part of HELLP criteria and can occur in AFLP from synthetic failure; it does not preferentially identify either (RANZCOG).

options.text: Maternal hypoglycaemia with raised ammonia and coagulopathy out of proportion to platelet drop
options.rationale: Hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia and a coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, raised INR) that is disproportionate to the relatively modest thrombocytopenia are highly suggestive of acute fatty liver of pregnancy, reflecting hepatic synthetic failure rather than microangiopathic haemolysis (Swansea criteria; RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital Vic).
source_refs: Swansea criteria (Knight et al.) for acute fatty liver of pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy

options.text: Serum urate above the upper limit of normal in the third trimester
options.rationale: Hyperuricaemia is associated with pre-eclampsia/HELLP and not specific to AFLP; it does not discriminate (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a hepatic synthetic failure syndrome of the third trimester. Distinguishing features versus HELLP include hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia, encephalopathy and a coagulopathy that exceeds what platelet count alone would explain. Definitive treatment for both is prompt delivery with maternal stabilisation (Swansea criteria; RANZCOG).
key_points: Swansea criteria: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin, hypoglycaemia, urate, leucocytosis, ascites/bright liver, transaminitis, ammonia, AKI, coagulopathy, microvesicular steatosis (>=6 of 14)
Maternal stabilisation focuses on glucose, coagulopathy reversal and timely delivery
Consider LCHAD deficiency screening in the infant after maternal AFLP
Multidisciplinary management with hepatology and intensive care
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia and hyperammonaemia in a third-trimester woman = think AFLP, not HELLP.

sources.label: Swansea criteria (Knight et al.) for acute fatty liver of pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Vic: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy
